In GitBucket, 
for ssh access, `.git` is required.
for http(s) access, GitBucket's formal http(s) URL format is `http://server:port/git/owner/repo.git`. 
But it also can access with:

- `http://server:port/git/owner/repo`
- `http://server:port/owner/repo.git`

But it can't access by `http://server:port/owner/repo`. Then it always add `.git`.

Recently, I found problem with git-2.12. I wrote it in https://github.com/gitbucket/gitbucket/issues/1552.

In GitBucket-4.12, `http://server:port/owner/repo.git` returns redirect to `http://server:port/git/owner/repo.git`. git-2.12 doesn't allow redirect url.
 so I will rewrite `format_path` such as:
- never call `super(...).format_path`
- returns `ssh://user@server:port/owner/repo.git` for rw=True
- returns `http://server:port/git/owner/repo.git` for rw=False

See https://naif.jpl.nasa.gov/pub/naif/toolkit_docs/C/cspice/dafgsr_c.html, specifically https://naif.jpl.nasa.gov/pub/naif/toolkit_docs/C/cspice/dafgsr_c.html#Exceptions

This routine retrieves a vector of double precision values from [beg:end], where beg and end are limited to 1 and 128, respectively.  So if you ask for beg=1 and end=10, or beg=11 and end=20, you only get 10 values, and it puts item beg (one-based) into data[0], and item end (again, one-based) into data[1+end-beg].  So the `data = stypes.emptyDoubleVector(125)` could be `data = stypes.emptyDoubleVector(1+end-beg)`

Now that I look at it again, the data vector should be sized to 128, not to 125; I was assuming the caller would be getting only summary record data, which exclude the first three bytes.  However, there is nothing stopping the caller from retrieving all 128 values.

The dafgsr_c internal C code protects the user from bad beg:end pairs e.g. overflow as in beg=1, end=1280.  Hmm, maybe there should also be some intelligence overflow about in the Python wrapper.

There is also the issue that the beg:end interval is specified in 1-based form; changing that to be begminus1:end in the Python code resulting in begminus1+1:end here would be Pythonic.  However, since NAIF chose not to do that when they wrote the C wrapper, it makes sense to continue that choice for the Python wrapper.  Otherwise, the NAIF CSPICE documentation, which is all the SpiceyPy user has, would become misleading for that SpiceyPy user.

I'm glad you asked. The main point of this PR is to expose a setting that is obvious and easy to access during test to allow tests to substitute a public key retriever during tests. Previously, this was very challenging without using `mock.patch()` on some internal implementation detail of the library.

The only problem with my solution is that it expects the user to provide a concrete class that can be instantiated by the Flask plugin to serve as the public key retriever. I wanted to be able to pass keys into that class though and the best way to do that was to create a factory function that would create an anonymous class that contained the static keys that I wanted to use.

In fact, I think this is generally a better approach - to carefully whitelist what APIs are considered public and documented. Liberal `automodule` has caused us many problems in the IPython project, and is specifically why we are trying something else on newer projects like this one. Each public API should be carefully and deliberately considered before committing it to the public API documentation.

I just had an extra look without running the code, but as far as I remember, it's when you upload new records. First bibupload runs, and part of this fix ensures that a record which is in the root collection can be displayed even though webcoll haven't run. Now webcoll runs and assigns the record to a multiple restricted collections (the restricted root collection is authorised to guest).
Now you try to display the record, which means that ``check_user_can_view_record()`` is called. Without recreating the cache, the root collection cache does not contain the new record (even though webcoll have just assigned it). The other collection caches however do contain the record. The result is that the root collection is not included in the list of restricted collections that the record is part of, which means that a guest user (who is only authorised to the root restricted collection) will not be authorised to see the record because he/she doesn't have access to the other restricted collections.

All in all, this is a symptom fix for a much bigger issue of the underlying authorisation system and cache and search.

Yeah; there are two styles of caller here:

1. The process execution caller, which calls `ensure_remote_has_recursive` because it specifically knows that files are missing, and needs to know that action has actually been taken as a result of its call. In this case, a no-op `ensure_remote_has_recursive` (because a poorly configured `Store` has been passed) likely leads to an infinite loop of the executor asking for files to be uploaded and retrying the action. What we really want to do in this case is error hard, saying "Files were missing, they couldn't be uploaded".
2. The fs_util caller, who calls `ensure_remote_has_recursive` because it wants to proactively ensure this is the case.

I'd rather optimise for the first case, which is in meaningful complicated code, than the second case (where this boolean is actually very easy to create and pass around, and its scope is very limited).

You declare in tests how the should behaves, what is the API, and what is the contract.
